title: How to Distinguish Between Intuition and Hypervigilance
researcherTopic: observation without the observer, freedom from conditioning
metaDescription: Learn to distinguish the quiet signal of intuition from the loud alarm of hypervigilance. This article explores the somatic and psychological differences.
imageDescription: A Kintsugi bowl, repaired with shimmering gold, sits in the soft, warm light of early morning. The light illuminates the delicate cracks, transforming them into beautiful features. The scene evokes a sense of gentle healing and the beauty of imperfection.
htmlContent:
Jiddu Krishnamurti suggested that the highest form of intelligence is observation without evaluation, a state of pure witnessing. For those of us who have navigated the territory of complex trauma, this idea presents a significant challenge, because the nervous system has been conditioned to evaluate everything... every subtle shift in another’s tone, every unexpected sound, every internal sensation - as a potential threat. Discerning the two is not an intellectual exercise; it is the delicate, moment-to-moment work of learning to inhabit a body that is finally, truly safe. It is the work of a lifetime, a slow and patient turning towards the truth of our own embodied experience.

The Echo Chamber of Threat

Hypervigilance is not a character flaw or a personal failing; it is a brilliant, adaptive strategy that once ensured our survival in an environment where threat was a constant companion. From the perspective of Stephen Porges' Polyvagal Theory, the nervous system becomes stuck in a state of mobilization, a sympathetic charge that is always on the lookout for danger. The brain’s amygdala, its ancient threat-detection center, learned to fire at the slightest provocation, creating a powerful and immediate physiological response. Meanwhile, the prefrontal cortex, the seat of rational thought and regulation, was often bypassed, its calming influence overridden by the primal urgency of survival. The brain, in its relentless effort to keep us safe, became a prediction machine, scanning the horizon for any sign of impending danger, and it learned its lessons so well that it continues to run the same predictive software long after the original threat has passed. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen how this emerges as a constant, low-grade hum of anxiety, a sense that something is always about to go wrong. What emerges is the echo chamber of threat, where the body continues to react to a danger that exists only as a memory, a ghost in the machine. The nervous system, in its wisdom, does not easily forget. It holds the score, as Bessel van der Kolk so aptly put it, and it demands to be heard. We can try to reason with it, to tell it that we are safe now, but the body has its own logic, a logic that is not swayed by affirmations or positive thinking. It responds not to what we believe, but to what it senses in the here and now.

The Subtle Signal of Knowing

Intuition, on the other hand, speaks in a different language altogether. It is not the frantic, clanging alarm of hypervigilance, but a quiet, steady signal that arises from a place of deep inner attunement and interoception ~ the capacity to sense the internal state of the body. This form of knowing is associated with the insula, a region of the brain that integrates bodily sensations with our emotional and cognitive processes. In a regulated nervous system, the insula functions like a finely tuned instrument, allowing us to feel our way through the world with clarity. After trauma, however, its signals can be drowned out by the noise of the amygdala. Unlike the fear-based predictions of hypervigilance, which are almost always rooted in the past and project a catastrophic future, intuition is a present-moment phenomenon. A client once described this as the difference between a smoke detector and a compass; one shrieks at any sign of potential danger, real or imagined, while the other simply points the way. The work of healing is learning to recalibrate our internal instrumentation, to turn down the volume on the smoke detector just enough to hear the quiet, unerring guidance of the compass. That becomes not about silencing the alarm, but about developing the capacity to pause, to breathe, and to ask: what is this signal really telling me? Is this a ghost from the past, or a genuine guide for the present? Learning to distinguish between the two is a critical step in moving from a life defined by reaction to a life guided by reclaimed inner authority.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

For managing sensory overwhelm, a tool that often helps is Sony WH-1000XM5 Noise Canceling Headphones (paid link).

The Tyranny of the Urgent

Cultivating the Inner Witness

The Practice of Titration and Pendulation

For a nervous system patterned by trauma, directly confronting difficult sensations can be overwhelming, leading to re-traumatization rather than resolution. That becomes why the principles of titration and pendulation, drawn from somatic therapies like Somatic Experiencing, are so essential. Titration is the practice of touching into difficult sensations or memories in very small, manageable doses - just a drop at a time... rather than flooding the system. Pendulation is the complementary practice of gently shifting our attention between that small dose of difficulty and a place of resource or relative safety in the body. By oscillating between the two, we are basically teaching the nervous system that it can approach the stored trauma energy without being consumed by it. It learns that it can come close to the fire and then retreat to safety, building its capacity for self-regulation incrementally. This rhythmic dance between activation and settlement allows the trapped survival energy to be safely discharged and integrated, rather than remaining stuck and fueling the cycle of hypervigilance. It is a genuinely respectful way of working with the body, honoring its limits while gently expanding its window of tolerance. This is not about forcing a release, but about creating the conditions for one to happen organically.

The Role of Self-Compassion

The Body as a Tuning Fork

How, then, do we begin to distinguish between the voice of trauma and the voice of truth? The answer lies not in the mind, but in the body. The body is the tuning fork, the instrument that can tell us whether we are resonating with fear or with clarity. Hypervigilance has a distinct physical signature: a tightening in the chest, a clenching in the jaw, a shallowness of breath, a sense of being trapped or constricted. It is the feeling of being revved up, of being on high alert. Intuition, on the other hand, tends to feel more expansive, more grounded, more settled. It might consequence as a sense of warmth in the chest, a feeling of solidity in the gut, a deep, easy breath. It is the feeling of a quiet ‘yes’ or a gentle ‘no’ that resonates through the entire system. The work is to become a student of these subtle sensations, to learn the unique language of our own bodies. It turns out to be the essence of somatic inquiry, the practice of turning our attention inward and exploring the territory of our internal experience with curiosity and without judgment. We can even practice this formally: take a moment to sit, close your eyes, and bring your attention to the soles of your feet. Feel their connection to the ground. Then, slowly, scan your awareness up through your body, simply noticing sensations without needing to change them. This simple act of somatic scanning can begin to build the interoceptive pathways necessary to hear the body's quieter signals. It is about befriending the body, learning its dialects of fear and its whispers of wisdom. This road is not about finding quick answers, but about deepening our capacity for somatic listening.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

The Shift Underneath

Ultimately, the road from hypervigilance to intuition is a road of returning. It is a return to the body, a return to the present moment, and a return to a sense of wholeness that we may have never known was possible. It is not a linear path, but a spiral, one that circles back on itself again and again, each time with a little more awareness, a little more compassion. This process is fueled by neuroplasticity, the striking capacity of the brain to form new neural pathways and reorganize itself in response to new experiences. Every time we pause instead of reacting, every time we choose to listen to the body's quiet signal instead of the mind's loud alarm, we are laying down a new track. There will be moments when the old alarms ring with a deafening intensity, when the ghosts of the past feel plainly real. “this is hypervigilance, this is a memory” - and to offer ourselves a measure of kindness. It is about understanding that the nervous system is not trying to sabotage us; it is trying to protect us with the only tools it has. Our task is to gently, patiently, offer it new tools, new experiences of safety, new possibilities for response. It is the slow, painstaking work of rewiring a system that has been organized around threat, and reorganizing it around connection, around presence, around the quiet, unshakable truth of our own being. What emerges is the path of coming home to ourselves, not as a destination, but as a continuous practice of arrival. The tenderness we extend to the frightened parts of ourselves becomes the very ground from which a new, more integrated self can emerge.

I want to talk about something that most people don't understand about triggers. They're not just emotional. They're physical. Your body doesn't just "feel upset" when triggered - it launches a full physiological cascade. Cortisol spikes. Heart rate increases. Blood pressure rises. Digestion shuts down. Peripheral vision narrows. Your body is preparing for a threat that isn't there anymore.

This is why "just calm down" doesn't work. You can't talk yourself out of a physiological state. It's like telling someone having an asthma attack to "just breathe normally." The system is doing what it's doing. You have to work with it, not against it.

Deb Dana's work on the polyvagal ladder gives us a map for this. When you're triggered, you've dropped down the ladder - from ventral vagal (safe, social) to sympathetic (fight/flight) or dorsal vagal (freeze/collapse). The way back up isn't through the mind. It's through the body. Through breath. Through movement. Through co-regulation with a safe other. Through tiny, repeated experiences of "I survived that. I'm still here. The danger passed."

Seriously.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

Frequently Asked Questions

How can I tell if a gut feeling is intuition or just anxiety?

The primary distinction lies in the quality of the sensation. Anxiety and hypervigilance often feel constrictive, urgent, and loud, accompanied by physical tension and a racing mind focused on past negative experiences. Intuition, conversely, tends to feel more calm, clear, and expansive, like a quiet knowing or a gentle pull that arises from a grounded place in the present moment.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Necklace - Shift (paid link).

Can hypervigilance ever be useful?

While it is a deeply uncomfortable state, the underlying mechanism of hypervigilance - threat detection - is a vital survival function. The challenge in healing is not to eliminate this capacity, but to recalibrate it, so that it activates only in response to genuine, present-moment danger rather than being constantly triggered by echoes of the past. It's about turning the alarm system from a default 'on' to a state of readiness.
